preamble.summary: Proverbs 8 is Woman Wisdom's greatest speech — her magnum opus. She speaks in three movements: her public proclamation and moral credentials (vv1-21), her pre-existence and role in creation (vv22-31), and her final appeal (vv32-36). This is the theological summit of the book's first nine chapters. Wisdom is not merely good advice; she was present before the world was made, and she was the craftsman at God's side when He set the foundations of the earth.
preamble.remarkable: The cosmological hymn in verses 22-31 is one of the most theologically significant passages in the entire Hebrew Bible. Wisdom declares: 'The LORD possessed me at the beginning of His way, before His acts of old. From eternity I was set up, from the beginning, before the earth existed' (vv22-23). She was present when God established the heavens, drew the horizon on the face of the deep, set the fountains of the deep, decreed the boundaries of the sea, and marked out the foundations of the earth. Verse 30 then makes the astounding claim: 'I was beside Him as a master craftsman, and I was His delight day after day, playing before Him at all times.' The word amon ('master craftsman' or 'nursling, darling') is debated, but either reading is remarkable — Wisdom was either God's co-worker in creation or God's beloved child, playing in His presence. The image of Wisdom 'playing' (mesachaqet) before God introduces joy, delight, and even playfulness into the creation account. God did not create grimly; He created joyfully, with Wisdom dancing at His side.
preamble.friction: The key theological question: is Wisdom a divine attribute, a separate being, a literary personification, or a proto-hypostasis? The text does not resolve this. Early Christianity read Proverbs 8 christologically (Christ as the pre-existent Wisdom of God), as reflected in John 1:1-3 and Colossians 1:15-17. The Arian controversy of the 4th century focused intensely on verse 22: does 'the LORD possessed/created me' mean Wisdom (and therefore Christ) is a created being? The verb qanah can mean 'to create,' 'to acquire,' 'to possess,' or 'to beget' — each option carries different theological implications. The Hebrew text is genuinely ambiguous, and the ambiguity has fueled centuries of debate.
preamble.connections: Proverbs 8:22-31 is the primary background text for John 1:1-3 ('In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God'), Colossians 1:15-17 ('He is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation'), and Hebrews 1:2 ('through whom He made the world'). The creation imagery parallels Job 38 (God laying the earth's foundation, setting the sea's boundaries) and Genesis 1 (ordering the waters, establishing the heavens). Sirach 24 and Wisdom of Solomon 7-9 both expand on Proverbs 8's portrait of personified wisdom. The 'fear of the LORD' refrain in verse 13 ties back to 1:7 and forward to 9:10.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲלֹא־חָכְמָ֥ה תִקְרָ֑א וּ֝תְבוּנָ֗ה תִּתֵּ֥ן קוֹלָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Doth not wisdom cry? and understanding put forth her voice?
verses.rendering:
Does not wisdom call out?
Does not understanding raise her voice?
translator_notes: The rhetorical question expects an emphatic 'Yes!' Wisdom is already calling — the question is whether anyone is listening. The parallel between chokmah ('wisdom') and tevunah ('understanding') establishes them as aspects of the same reality. The verbs tiqra ('calls out') and titten qolah ('gives her voice') echo 1:20-21, framing chapters 1-9 with Wisdom's public proclamation.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּרֹאשׁ־מְרוֹמִ֥ים עֲלֵי־דָ֑רֶךְ בֵּ֖ית נְתִיב֣וֹת נִצָּֽבָה׃
verses.text_kjv: She standeth in the top of high places, by the way in the places of the paths.
verses.rendering:
On the heights beside the road,
at the crossroads, she takes her stand.
translator_notes: Wisdom stations herself at the rosh meromim ('top of the heights') and bet netivot ('the meeting-place of paths, the crossroads'). She chooses the places of maximum visibility and decision — where travelers must choose which direction to go. Her position is strategic: she speaks where choices are made.

verses.text_hebrew: לְיַד־שְׁעָרִ֥ים לְפִי־קָ֑רֶת מְב֖וֹא פְתָחִ֣ים תָּרֹֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: She crieth at the gates, at the entry of the city, at the coming in at the doors.
verses.rendering:
Beside the gates, at the entrance to the city,
at the approach of the doorways, she cries aloud:
translator_notes: Three locations converge: she'arim ('gates,' the legal and commercial center), pi qaret ('the mouth of the city, the entrance'), and mevo petachim ('the approach of the doorways'). She is everywhere public life happens. Unlike the forbidden woman who lurks at corners in darkness (7:12), Wisdom stands openly at the most visible, most public locations.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲלֵיכֶ֣ם אִישִׁ֣ים אֶקְרָ֑א וְ֝קוֹלִ֗י אֶל־בְּנֵ֥י אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Unto you, O men, I call; and my voice is to the sons of man.
verses.rendering:
'To you, O people, I call,
and my voice reaches out to all humanity.
translator_notes: The address is universal: ishim ('men, people') and bene adam ('sons of Adam, human beings'). Wisdom is not speaking to Israel alone but to all humanity. Her claim is cosmic, not tribal.

verses.text_hebrew: הָבִ֣ינוּ פְתָאיִ֣ם עָרְמָ֑ה וּ֝כְסִילִ֗ים הָבִ֥ינוּ לֵֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: O ye simple, understand wisdom: and, ye fools, be ye of an understanding heart.
verses.rendering:
Learn shrewdness, you naive ones;
you fools, gain sense.
translator_notes: Wisdom addresses the petayim ('naive') and kesilim ('fools') directly. She does not write them off but offers them what they lack: ormah ('shrewdness, cunning') and lev ('heart, sense'). She provides the very thing the young man in chapter 7 was missing — chasar lev ('lacking heart').

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁ֭מְעוּ כִּֽי־נְגִידִ֣ים אֲדַבֵּ֑ר וּמִפְתַּ֥ח שְׂ֝פָתַ֗י מֵישָׁרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear; for I will speak of excellent things; and the opening of my lips shall be right things.
verses.rendering:
Listen, for I speak of noble things,
and from my lips come what is right.
translator_notes: Negidim ('noble things, princely things, things worthy of rulers') and mesharim ('right things, upright things, equitable things'). Wisdom's speech content matches her authority — she does not gossip or deceive but speaks what is worthy and straight.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־אֱ֭מֶת יֶהְגֶּ֣ה חִכִּ֑י וְתוֹעֲבַ֖ת שְׂפָתַ֣י רֶֽשַׁע׃
verses.text_kjv: For my mouth shall speak truth; and wickedness is an abomination to my lips.
verses.rendering:
For my mouth utters truth,
and wickedness is detestable to my lips.
translator_notes: Emet ('truth, reliability, faithfulness') is what Wisdom's chikki ('palate, mouth') yehgeh ('murmurs, meditates, utters'). Wickedness is to'avat sefatay ('an abomination to my lips'). The contrast with the forbidden woman is total: her mouth drips honey that leads to wormwood; Wisdom's mouth speaks truth that leads to life.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּצֶ֥דֶק כָּל־אִמְרֵי־פִ֑י אֵ֥ין בָּ֝הֶ֗ם נִפְתָּ֥ל וְעִקֵּֽשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: All the words of my mouth are in righteousness; there is nothing froward or perverse in them.
verses.rendering:
All the words of my mouth are righteous;
none of them are twisted or crooked.
translator_notes: Be-tsedeq ('in righteousness') qualifies every word (kol imre pi, 'all the words of my mouth'). There is nothing niftal ('twisted, tortuous') or iqqesh ('crooked, perverted') in them. The absence of crookedness is the defining marker: the forbidden woman's speech was smooth; Wisdom's speech is straight.

verses.text_hebrew: כֻּלָּ֣ם נְ֭כֹחִים לַמֵּבִ֑ין וִֽ֝ישָׁרִ֗ים לְמֹ֣צְאֵי דָֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: They are all plain to him that understandeth, and right to them that find knowledge.
verses.rendering:
They are all straightforward to the one who understands,
and right to those who have found knowledge.
translator_notes: Wisdom's words are nekhochim ('straight, plain, right') to the mevin ('one who discerns') and yesharim ('upright, right') to those who have already found da'at ('knowledge'). Understanding enables understanding — the more you know, the more Wisdom's words make sense. This is the opposite of the fool's spiral, where ignorance breeds more ignorance.

verses.text_hebrew: קְחוּ־מוּסָרִ֥י וְאַל־כָּ֑סֶף וְ֝דַ֗עַת מֵחָר֥וּץ נִבְחָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: Receive my instruction, and not silver; and knowledge rather than choice gold.
verses.rendering:
Receive my discipline rather than silver,
and knowledge rather than finest gold.
translator_notes: The economic comparison from 3:14-15 returns with new force: choose musari ('my discipline') over keseph ('silver') and da'at ('knowledge') over charutz nivchar ('chosen/finest gold'). Wisdom herself, not just the father, now makes the value claim.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־טוֹבָ֣ה חָ֭כְמָה מִפְּנִינִ֑ים וְכָל־חֲ֝פָצִ֗ים לֹ֣א יִשְׁווּ־בָֽהּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For wisdom is better than rubies; and all the things that may be desired are not to be compared to it.
verses.rendering:
For wisdom is better than corals,
and no treasure can compare with her.
translator_notes: Virtually identical to 3:15. The repetition of peninim ('corals/rubies/pearls') and kol chafatsim ('all desirable things') unable to equal wisdom creates a refrain throughout the first nine chapters — wisdom's incomparable value is stated and restated until it becomes the book's baseline assumption.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲ֭נִי חָכְמָ֣ה שָׁכַ֣נְתִּי עָרְמָ֑ה וְדַ֖עַת מְזִמּ֣וֹת אֶמְצָֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: I wisdom dwell with prudence, and find out knowledge of witty inventions.
verses.rendering:
I, Wisdom, dwell with shrewdness,
and I possess knowledge and foresight.
translator_notes: Wisdom speaks in the first person with authority: ani chokmah ('I, Wisdom'). She shakanti ('I dwell, I reside') with ormah ('shrewdness, cunning, craftiness') and she finds (emtsa) da'at mezimmot ('knowledge of foresight, knowledge of planning'). Wisdom is not naive — she is shrewd, strategic, and perceptive.

verses.text_hebrew: יִרְאַ֣ת יְהוָה֮ שְׂנֹ֢את רָ֡ע גֵּ֘אָ֤ה וְגָא֨וֹן ׀ וְדֶ֣רֶךְ רָ֭ע וּפִ֨י תַהְפֻּכ֬וֹת שָׂנֵֽאתִי׃
verses.text_kjv: The fear of the LORD is to hate evil: pride, and arrogancy, and the evil way, and the froward mouth, do I hate.
verses.rendering:
The fear of the LORD is hatred of evil.
Pride and arrogance and the evil way
and the mouth of perversity — I hate.
verses.expanded_rendering: Wisdom defines yirat YHWH ('the fear of the LORD') not as an emotion but as a moral orientation: seno't ra ('hatred of evil'). To fear the LORD is to hate what He hates. Wisdom then catalogs what she hates: ge'ah ('pride, exaltation'), ga'on ('arrogance, haughtiness'), derekh ra ('the evil way'), and pi tahpukhot ('the mouth of perversity, speech that inverts reality'). The list covers internal disposition (pride), public posture (arrogance), life-direction (the evil way), and speech (perverted mouth). Wisdom's hatred is not arbitrary but structural — she opposes everything that distorts the moral order God embedded in creation. This verse is the ethical center of the chapter: before Wisdom describes her cosmic origins, she establishes her moral identity. She is not a neutral force but a partisan of righteousness.
translator_notes: The definition of the fear of the LORD as 'hatred of evil' is one of the sharpest formulations in the book. It converts the fear of the LORD from a private devotional posture into a public moral stance — you cannot claim to fear the LORD while tolerating what He finds detestable.

verses.text_hebrew: לִֽי־עֵ֭צָה וְתוּשִׁיָּ֑ה אֲנִ֥י בִ֝ינָ֗ה לִ֣י גְבוּרָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Counsel is mine, and sound wisdom: I am understanding; I have strength.
verses.rendering:
Counsel is mine, and sound wisdom;
I am understanding; strength belongs to me.
translator_notes: Wisdom claims four possessions: etsah ('counsel, advice, strategy'), tushiyyah ('sound wisdom, effective counsel'), binah ('understanding' — she does not merely have it; she is it), and gevurah ('strength, might, power'). Wisdom is not weak or merely theoretical — she has the power to accomplish what she advises.

verses.text_hebrew: בִּ֭י מְלָכִ֣ים יִמְלֹ֑כוּ וְ֝רוֹזְנִ֗ים יְחֹ֣קְקוּ צֶֽדֶק׃
verses.text_kjv: By me kings reign, and princes decree justice.
verses.rendering:
By me kings reign,
and rulers decree what is just.
translator_notes: Wisdom claims political authority: bi ('by me, through me') melakhim yimlokhu ('kings reign'). She is the source of legitimate governance. Rozenim ('rulers, princes') yechoqequ tsedeq ('decree, inscribe, enact righteousness'). Without wisdom, kings cannot rule justly; with her, their decrees embody tsedeq ('righteousness'). Political authority is derivative of wisdom, not independent of it.

verses.text_hebrew: בִּ֭י שָׂרִ֣ים יָשֹׂ֑רוּ וּ֝נְדִיבִ֗ים כָּל־שֹׁ֥פְטֵי צֶֽדֶק׃
verses.text_kjv: By me princes rule, and nobles, even all the judges of the earth.
verses.rendering:
By me officials govern,
and nobles — all who judge rightly.
translator_notes: The claim extends from kings (v15) to sarim ('officials, princes') and nedivim ('nobles, generous ones') and kol shofte tsedeq ('all judges of righteousness'). Every level of governance depends on wisdom. The judicial system itself requires her presence to function justly.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲנִ֣י אֹהֲבַ֣י אֵהָ֑ב וּ֝מְשַׁחֲרַ֗י יִמְצָאֻֽנְנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: I love them that love me; and those that seek me early shall find me.
verses.rendering:
I love those who love me,
and those who seek me diligently find me.
translator_notes: Wisdom offers reciprocal love: ani ohavay ehav ('I, those who love me, I love'). She also promises findability: meshacharay yimtsa'ununni ('those who seek me early/eagerly will find me'). Unlike the forbidden woman who hunts the unwary (7:12), Wisdom rewards those who seek her. The seeking must be meshachar ('at dawn, eagerly, diligently') — casual inquiry will not suffice.

verses.text_hebrew: עֹֽשֶׁר־וְכָב֥וֹד אִתִּ֑י ה֥וֹן עָ֝תֵ֗ק וּצְדָקָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Riches and honour are with me; yea, durable riches and righteousness.
verses.rendering:
Riches and honor are with me,
enduring wealth and righteousness.
translator_notes: Osher ('riches'), kavod ('honor'), hon ateq ('enduring wealth, ancient wealth') and tsedaqah ('righteousness'). The pairing of material prosperity with moral integrity is characteristic of Proverbs — true wealth includes righteousness, and righteousness includes a form of wealth.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, justice, rightness, vindication; conformity to God's moral order and right relationship within the community
key_terms.note: Tsedaqah is not merely moral correctness but relational rightness — being in proper alignment with God, with community, and with the created order. In Proverbs it often appears alongside wealth, suggesting that true prosperity and true righteousness are inseparable.

verses.text_hebrew: ט֣וֹב פִּ֭רְיִי מֵחָר֣וּץ וּמִפָּ֑ז וּ֝תְבוּאָתִ֗י מִכֶּ֥סֶף נִבְחָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: My fruit is better than gold, yea, than fine gold; and my revenue than choice silver.
verses.rendering:
My fruit is better than gold, even fine gold,
and my yield than choice silver.
translator_notes: Wisdom's piryi ('my fruit') and tevu'ati ('my yield, my produce') surpass charutz ('fine gold'), paz ('pure gold, refined gold'), and keseph nivchar ('choice silver'). The agricultural metaphor (fruit, yield) applied to wisdom implies that she produces ongoing returns, not a one-time payment.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאֹֽרַח־צְדָקָ֥ה אֲהַלֵּ֑ך בְּ֝ת֗וֹךְ נְתִיב֥וֹת מִשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: I lead in the way of righteousness, in the midst of the paths of judgment:
verses.rendering:
I walk in the path of righteousness,
in the midst of the paths of justice,
translator_notes: Wisdom walks — ahalekh ('I walk, I go') — in the orach tsedaqah ('path of righteousness') and betokh netivot mishpat ('in the midst of the paths of justice'). She is not merely a guide who points the way; she walks the road herself. Following wisdom means walking where she walks.

verses.text_hebrew: לְהַנְחִ֣יל אֹהֲבַ֣י יֵ֑שׁ וְאֹצְרֹ֖תֵיהֶ֣ם אֲמַלֵּֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: That I may cause those that love me to inherit substance; and I will fill their treasures.
verses.rendering:
granting substance to those who love me
and filling their treasuries.
translator_notes: Lehanchil ohavay yesh ('to cause those who love me to inherit substance, reality, something') — the word yesh means 'existence, substance, what is real.' Wisdom provides not illusion but reality. She also fills (amalle) their otseroteihem ('their treasuries, their storehouses'). The gifts of wisdom are concrete and lasting.

verses.text_hebrew: יְֽהוָ֗ה קָ֭נָנִי רֵאשִׁ֣ית דַּרְכּ֑וֹ קֶ֖דֶם מִפְעָלָ֣יו מֵאָֽז׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD possessed me in the beginning of his way, before his works of old.
verses.rendering:
The LORD brought me forth as the first of His way,
before His works of old.
verses.expanded_rendering: The cosmological hymn begins with the most debated verse in the chapter. YHWH qanani reshit darkho ('the LORD qanani — at the beginning of His way'). The verb qanah has four possible meanings, and each produces a different theological claim. (1) 'Possessed me' — Wisdom was always God's possession. (2) 'Created me' — Wisdom was the first thing God made, before everything else. (3) 'Acquired me' — God obtained Wisdom at the start. (4) 'Begot me' — Wisdom was generated from God's own being. The LXX translated it as ektisen ('created'), which fueled the Arian argument that the pre-existent Logos was a created being. The word reshit ('beginning, first, chief') is the same word from Genesis 1:1 (bereshit, 'in the beginning') and from Proverbs 1:7 (reshit da'at, 'the beginning of knowledge'). Wisdom claims priority over all creation — she was there before anything else existed. Qedem mif'alaw me'az ('before His works from of old') pushes the claim further back: before God acted, Wisdom was already present.
translator_notes: We render qanani as 'brought me forth' to capture the generative force of the verb without committing to either 'created' or 'possessed.' The Hebrew is ambiguous, and we preserve the ambiguity rather than resolving it. The theological stakes of this translation choice are immense — entire christological debates have turned on whether this verb means 'created' or 'begot.'

key_terms.semantic_range: created, acquired, possessed, begot, brought forth; from qanah, a verb of origin and ownership
key_terms.note: Qanah in Genesis 4:1 describes Eve 'acquiring/producing' Cain. In Deuteronomy 32:6 it describes God as the one who 'made/acquired/begot' Israel. In Proverbs 8:22, the meaning determines whether Wisdom is eternal (possessed) or primordially originated (created/begotten). The ambiguity is genuine.

verses.text_hebrew: מֵ֭עוֹלָם נִסַּ֥כְתִּי מֵרֹ֗אשׁ מִקַּדְמֵי־אָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: I was set up from everlasting, from the beginning, or ever the earth was.
verses.rendering:
From eternity I was appointed,
from the beginning, before the earth existed.
translator_notes: Me-olam ('from eternity, from the distant past') nissakti ('I was set up, appointed, installed, poured out'). The verb nasakh may mean 'to install' (as a king is installed) or 'to weave' or 'to pour out' — each suggests a different kind of origin. Me-rosh ('from the first, from the head/beginning') miqqadme arets ('before the antiquity of the earth'). The earth itself is ancient, but Wisdom predates it.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאֵין־תְּהֹמ֥וֹת חוֹלָ֑לְתִּי בְּאֵ֥ין מַ֝עְיָנ֗וֹת נִכְבַּדֵּי־מָֽיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: When there were no depths, I was brought forth; when there were no fountains abounding with water.
verses.rendering:
When there were no ocean depths, I was brought forth;
when there were no springs heavy with water.
translator_notes: Be-ein tehomot ('when there were no deeps, no primordial oceans') cholalti ('I was brought forth, I was birthed, I writhed into being'). The verb chul/chil means 'to writhe, to travail, to be born' — it is explicitly birth language. The tehomot ('deeps') echo Genesis 1:2 (tehom, 'the deep'). Before the primordial ocean existed, Wisdom was already born. The ma'yanot nikbadde mayim ('springs heavy/abundant with water') represent the underground water sources — even the hidden waters postdate Wisdom.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּטֶ֣רֶם הָרִ֣ים הָטְבָּ֑עוּ לִפְנֵ֖י גְבָע֣וֹת חוֹלָֽלְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: Before the mountains were settled, before the hills was I brought forth:
verses.rendering:
Before the mountains were sunk into place,
before the hills, I was brought forth —
translator_notes: Beterem harim hotba'u ('before mountains were sunk, were set firmly') — the verb tava ('to sink, to be pressed down, to be immersed') describes mountains as driven into the earth like posts into the ground. Lifne geva'ot cholalti ('before hills I was birthed'). Mountains represent the most ancient, most permanent features of the landscape; Wisdom is older than all of them.

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־לֹ֣א עָ֭שָׂה אֶ֣רֶץ וְחוּצ֑וֹת וְ֝רֹ֗אשׁ עָפְר֥וֹת תֵּבֵֽל׃
verses.text_kjv: While as yet he had not made the earth, nor the fields, nor the highest part of the dust of the world.
verses.rendering:
before He had made the earth or the fields,
or the first dust of the world.
translator_notes: Ad lo asah erets ('while He had not yet made the earth') and chutsot ('open spaces, fields') and rosh afrot tevel ('the first/chief dust particles of the world'). Even dust — the most basic building material of creation — postdates Wisdom. She was there before the raw materials existed.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּהֲכִינ֣וֹ שָׁ֭מַיִם שָׁ֣ם אָ֑נִי בְּח֥וּקוֹ ח֝֗וּג עַל־פְּנֵ֥י תְהֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: When he prepared the heavens, I was there: when he set a compass upon the face of the depth:
verses.rendering:
When He established the heavens, I was there;
when He inscribed the horizon on the face of the deep,
translator_notes: Now Wisdom describes not her priority but her presence during creation. Bahakhino shamayim ('when He established the heavens') sham ani ('there I was'). The two words sham ani ('I was there') are the chapter's quiet thunder — Wisdom witnessed everything. Bechuqo chug al pene tehom ('when He inscribed a circle/horizon on the face of the deep') — God drew the boundary between sky and sea, and Wisdom watched.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאַמְּצ֣וֹ שְׁחָקִ֣ים מִמָּ֑עַל בַּ֝עֲז֗וֹז עִינ֥וֹת תְּהוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: When he established the clouds above: when he strengthened the fountains of the deep:
verses.rendering:
when He made firm the skies above,
when He established the fountains of the deep,
translator_notes: Be-ammetso shechaqim ('when He strengthened/made firm the clouds/skies above') and ba'azoz enot tehom ('when He made strong the springs of the deep'). Both the upper waters (sky) and the lower waters (deep springs) were set in place by God, with Wisdom present.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּשׂוּמ֤וֹ לַיָּ֨ם ׀ חֻקּ֗וֹ וּ֭מַיִם לֹ֣א יַֽעַבְרוּ־פִ֑יו בְּ֝חוּק֗וֹ מ֣וֹסְדֵי אָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: When he gave to the sea his decree, that the waters should not pass his commandment: when he appointed the foundations of the earth:
verses.rendering:
when He set a boundary for the sea
so that the waters would not transgress His command,
when He marked out the foundations of the earth —
translator_notes: Besumo layyam chuqqo ('when He placed for the sea its boundary/decree') — the sea's limit is both physical (shoreline) and legal (decree). The waters lo ya'avru piv ('will not cross His mouth/command'). In Job 38:8-11, God gives the sea the same command: 'Here shall your proud waves be stopped.' Bechuqo mosde arets ('when He marked out the foundations of the earth') — the earth has structural supports, and God laid them.

verses.text_hebrew: וָאֶהְיֶ֣ה אֶצְל֣וֹ אָמ֑וֹן וָאֶהְיֶ֣ה שַׁ֭עֲשֻׁעִים י֤וֹם ׀ י֗וֹם מְשַׂחֶ֥קֶת לְפָנָ֗יו בְּכָל־עֵֽת׃
verses.text_kjv: Then I was by him, as one brought up with him: and I was daily his delight, rejoicing always before him;
verses.rendering:
then I was beside Him as a master craftsman.
I was His delight day after day,
playing in His presence at all times,
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the theological apex of the chapter and one of the most discussed verses in the Hebrew Bible. Va'ehyeh etslo amon ('and I was beside Him, an amon'). The word amon has been translated as 'master craftsman' (from aman, 'to be skilled, to be firm'), 'nursling' or 'darling child' (from aman, 'to nurse, to rear'), or 'faithful one' (from aman, 'to be faithful'). If 'master craftsman,' Wisdom is God's co-worker in creation, the skilled artisan who executed the divine design. If 'nursling' or 'darling,' Wisdom is God's beloved child, delighting in His presence while He works. Either reading is profound. Va'ehyeh sha'ashu'im yom yom ('and I was His delight day after day') — Wisdom brought God pleasure throughout the creation process. Mesachaqet lefanav bekhol et ('playing before Him at all times') — the verb sachaq ('to laugh, to play, to sport') introduces joy and playfulness into the creation account. Creation was not grim labor but delighted play. Wisdom danced and laughed before God as He built the world. The image shatters every image of God as a dour, joyless Creator — He made the world accompanied by laughter.
translator_notes: We render amon as 'master craftsman' as the primary reading, acknowledging that 'darling child' is equally defensible. The choice of 'craftsman' connects to the broader theme of wisdom as skill (chokmah as artisanship in Exodus 31:3) and to the image of God as builder throughout vv27-29. But the 'darling child' reading explains the play language of verse 30b more naturally — a craftsman works; a child plays.

key_terms.semantic_range: master craftsman, artisan, skilled worker; OR nursling, ward, darling child; OR faithful one, trustworthy companion
key_terms.note: The ambiguity of amon may be intentional — Wisdom is simultaneously God's co-worker and God's delight, both the skilled instrument of creation and the beloved companion who brings God joy. The word resists resolution into a single meaning.

verses.text_hebrew: מְ֭שַׂחֶקֶת בְּתֵבֵ֣ל אַרְצ֑וֹ וְשַׁעֲשֻׁעַ֖י אֶת־בְּנֵ֣י אָדָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Rejoicing in the habitable part of his earth; and my delights were with the sons of men.
verses.rendering:
playing in His inhabited world,
and my delight was with the children of humanity.
translator_notes: Mesachaqet betevel artso ('playing in the world of His earth, in His inhabited land') — Wisdom's play extends from God's presence (v30) into the created world itself. The earth is not merely God's construction project but Wisdom's playground. Vesha'ashu'ay et bene adam ('and my delights were with the sons of Adam') — the most stunning claim of all: Wisdom delights in human beings. Her joy is not only in God's presence but in human company. Humanity is not an afterthought of creation but the object of Wisdom's specific delight.

verses.text_hebrew: וְעַתָּ֣ה בָ֭נִים שִׁמְעוּ־לִ֑י וְ֝אַשְׁרֵ֗י דְּרָכַ֥י יִשְׁמֹֽרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Now therefore hearken unto me, O ye children: for blessed are they that keep my ways.
verses.rendering:
So now, children, listen to me:
how fortunate are those who keep my ways!
translator_notes: After the cosmic vision, the practical appeal. Ve-attah ('so now') transitions from creation theology to present command. Ashre derakhay yishmoru ('fortunate are those who guard my ways'). The ashre ('fortunate') from 3:13 returns — the blessing of wisdom-keeping is reaffirmed after the cosmic revelation.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁמְע֖וּ מוּסָ֥ר וַחֲכָ֗מוּ וְאַל־תִּפְרָֽעוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear instruction, and be wise, and refuse it not.
verses.rendering:
Hear discipline and become wise;
do not neglect it.
translator_notes: The imperative sequence — shim'u ('hear'), chakhamu ('become wise'), al tifra'u ('do not neglect, do not let go') — is brisk and direct. After thirty-one verses of cosmic vision, the command is simple: listen, learn, hold on.

verses.text_hebrew: אַשְׁרֵ֣י אָ֭דָם שֹׁמֵ֣עַ לִ֑י לִשְׁקֹ֥ד עַל־דַּ֝לְתֹתַ֗י י֤וֹם ׀ י֬וֹם לִשְׁמֹ֗ר מְזוּזֹ֥ת פְּתָחָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed is the man that heareth me, watching daily at my gates, waiting at the posts of my doors.
verses.rendering:
How fortunate is the person who listens to me,
watching daily at my gates,
waiting beside my doorposts.
translator_notes: The image of lishqod al dalthotay yom yom ('watching at my doors day after day') and lishmor mezuzot petachay ('guarding the doorposts of my entrances') presents the wise person as a devoted attendant. The mezuzot ('doorposts') echo Deuteronomy 6:9, where God's commands are written on the doorposts. Now Wisdom's own doorposts are the destination. The devoted student stands at her door the way a servant stands at a master's gate — ready, attentive, unwilling to miss a word.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֣י מֹ֭צְאִי מָצָ֣א חַיִּ֑ים וַיָּ֥פֶק רָ֝צ֗וֹן מֵיְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For whoso findeth me findeth life, and shall obtain favour of the LORD.
verses.rendering:
For whoever finds me finds life
and obtains favor from the LORD.
translator_notes: Motse'i matsa chayyim ('the one who finds me has found life') — wisdom and life are equated. Finding wisdom is not a step toward life; it is the finding of life itself. Vayyafeq ratson me-YHWH ('and draws forth favor from the LORD') — the verb yafaq ('to bring forth, to obtain, to draw out') suggests that God's favor flows naturally to the one who finds wisdom.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭חֹטְאִי חֹמֵ֣ס נַפְשׁ֑וֹ כָּל־מְ֝שַׂנְאַ֗י אָ֣הֲבוּ מָֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: But he that sinneth against me wrongeth his own soul: all they that hate me love death.
verses.rendering:
But whoever misses me harms himself;
all who hate me love death.
translator_notes: Wisdom's final word is the starkest binary in the book. Chot'i ('the one who misses me, the one who sins against me') — the root chata means 'to miss the mark, to go wrong.' Missing wisdom is self-harm (chomes nafsho, 'does violence to his own soul'). Kol mesan'ay ahevu mavet ('all who hate me love death'). The sentence is absolute: there is no third option between finding wisdom (and life) and hating wisdom (and loving death). The one who rejects wisdom has, whether knowingly or not, chosen death as a lover. This is Woman Wisdom's final word before the climactic confrontation of chapter 9.
